title: The K-Means Algorithm, Step by Step
concept: K-Means repeats a two-step loop — assign points to their nearest centroid, then move each centroid to the average of its assigned points — until the centroids stop moving.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In K-Means, what decides which cluster a data point joins?

options.text: Its distance to each centroid
The date it was added to the dataset
A vote among nearby points

exercises.explain: Right! A point always joins whichever centroid is closest to it.

cards.title: Guess, check, adjust, repeat
cards.body: Imagine guessing the center of a crowd by picking a random spot, then noticing everyone is actually a bit to your left. You'd shuffle left. Then check again. Then shuffle again. Eventually you'd settle near the true center — that repeated guess-and-adjust is exactly how K-Means finds its centroids.

cards.title: The two-step loop
cards.body: K-Means repeats two steps until nothing changes: 1) Assign — give each point to its nearest centroid. 2) Update — move each centroid to the average position of all points now assigned to it. Then repeat step 1 with the moved centroids.

cards.title: A magnet pulled toward its crowd
cards.body: After every round of assignments, each centroid acts like a magnet that gets yanked toward the average position of its own group — not toward any single point, but toward the center of mass of everyone currently standing near it. Then people re-check who's now closest, and the magnets get yanked again.

cards.title: Walking through one update
cards.body: Say a centroid's cluster currently has three points: (1,1), (1,3), and (4,2). The new centroid position is the average of each coordinate: x = (1+1+4)/3 = 2, y = (1+3+2)/3 = 2. The centroid moves to (2, 2), then every point is re-checked against all centroids' new positions.

cards.title: It doesn't loop forever
cards.body: You might worry this assign-and-update cycle runs endlessly. In reality, it converges — once an update round barely moves the centroids (or points stop switching clusters), the algorithm stops. Most real runs settle within a few dozen iterations, not thousands.

cards.title: Assign step vs. update step
cards.body: Two very different jobs inside one loop:

right.text:
Centroids move to the
average of their points

exercises.prompt: What happens during the 'update' step of K-Means?

options.text: Each centroid moves to the average position of the points currently assigned to it
New centroids are added to the dataset
Points are deleted if they don't fit a cluster well
The value of K is increased by one

exercises.explain: Correct! The update step recalculates each centroid as the average of its assigned points.

exercises.prompt: True or false: K-Means alternates between assigning points to centroids and moving centroids, until the centroids stop changing much.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! That assign-then-update loop, repeated until stable, is the whole algorithm.

exercises.sentence: After points are assigned, each centroid moves to the ___ position of its assigned points.
exercises.explain: The centroid is recalculated as the average (mean) of all points currently in its cluster.

exercises.prompt: Which process below correctly describes the K-Means loop?

options.text: Assign every point to its nearest centroid, then move each centroid to the average of its points, repeating until stable
Sort all points from smallest to largest, then cut them into K equal-sized chunks
Randomly shuffle points between clusters until a human is satisfied

exercises.explain: Right! Assign, then update, repeated until convergence — that's the K-Means loop.

exercises.prompt: A centroid has two points assigned to it: (0, 0) and (2, 4). Where should the centroid move during the update step?

options.text: (1, 2), the average of the two points' coordinates
(2, 4), the farthest of the two points
(0, 0), the closest of the two points
It stays wherever it started, centroids never move

exercises.explain: Exactly! Averaging x: (0+2)/2=1, and y: (0+4)/2=2, gives the new centroid position (1, 2).

exercises.prompt: After several rounds of K-Means, a data scientist notices that in the latest round, not a single point switched clusters and the centroids moved by less than 0.001 units. What should happen next?

options.text: The algorithm has converged and should stop — further rounds won't meaningfully change the result
K should be increased immediately to force more movement
The centroids should be reset to random positions and restarted
The loop must run exactly 100 times regardless of movement

exercises.explain: Exactly! When assignments and centroid positions stop meaningfully changing, K-Means has converged — that's the natural stopping point. Next: how to pick the right K in the first place!
